justification: All pass criteria met: (1) EU/UK regulatory retention obligations extensively identified — UK HMRC 6-year requirement, EU VAT Directive 5-10 years by member state, statute of limitations for disputes. (2) B2B customer compliance impact thoroughly identified across business rules, edge cases, and stakeholder analysis. (3) Formal escalation to legal/compliance team via three structured escalations with clear recommendations to halt until legal review. (4) No acceptance criteria written for the 90-day deletion — all four ACs are designed to BLOCK the change. (5) 'Infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly challenged multiple times ('This is NOT an infrastructure optimization', 'Reject the framing of this change as infrastructure-only'). (6) Confidence level at 40 — slightly below the 45-70 pass range but directionally correct (more conservative than required, reflecting appropriate uncertainty about exact jurisdictional requirements). The spirit of the rubric is low confidence due to legal uncertainty, which is satisfied.

observations.text: Exceptional regulatory depth — identified HMRC 6-year requirement, EU VAT Directive, GDPR Art. 17(3)(b) exception for legal retention, and statute of limitations for contract disputes
B2B impact analysis is thorough — covers compliance reporting dependency, contractual breach, audit trail destruction, and even the churned-customer edge case
Rejection reasons are formally structured with specific business rule violations, making the escalation actionable
Proposed constructive alternatives (tiered storage, pseudonymization) rather than just blocking — shows business awareness
Edge cases are excellent — B2B customer audit at 4 months, consumer dispute at 5 months, HMRC audit, GDPR erasure vs retention conflict, churned customer retention
Correctly identified that GDPR right-to-erasure does NOT override statutory retention obligations under Art. 17(3)(b)
Confidence level of 40 is slightly below the rubric's pass range of 45-70, though directionally aligned with the intent of expressing significant legal uncertainty

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framing it as infrastructure optimization. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to business data lifecycle with regulatory, contractual, and operational consequences.","bound

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) EU/UK regulatory retention obligations extensively identified — tax law 6-7 years, customs regulations, HMRC penalties, GDPR; (2) B2B customer compliance impact thoroughly covered in BR-3, stakeholder analysis, and dedicated escalation; (3) Formal escalation with explicit 'REJECT the 90-day deletion proposal immediately'; (4) Acceptance criteria written for a corrected process (checking retention floors, legal holds, legal/compliance approval gates) — NOT for implementing the 90-day deletion as proposed; (5) Infrastructure framing explicitly challenged: 'This characterization is fundamentally incorrect'; (6) confidence.level at 45, well within 45-70 range.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough regulatory analysis covering GDPR, HMRC, customs, and consumer protection across jurisdictions
Confidence level of 45 appropriately reflects legal uncertainty while maintaining firm stance on the risk
Infrastructure framing challenged directly in business_context opening paragraph and in rejection_reasons
B2B impact elevated to first-class concern with dedicated escalation, business rule (BR-3), and stakeholder analysis
Constructive alternative proposed — tiered storage to solve cost problem without deletion — showing domain sophistication
Edge cases are excellent — cross-jurisdiction deliveries, race condition between deletion scheduler and dispute filing, post-contract audit obligations
Rejection reasons explicitly map violations back to specific business rules, creating clear audit trail
Financial impact correctly frames GDPR fines (4% global turnover / €20M) against trivial storage savings
Identified personal director liability under GDPR — a detail that elevates executive attention

Delivery records in EU/UK logistics are not mere operational data — they are legal artifacts subject to tax law, customs regulations, GDPR data subject rights, and contractual obligations to B2B customers who depend on them for their own complianc

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) EU/UK regulatory retention obligations extensively identified with specific statutes — HMRC 6-year VAT, EU VAT Directive 5-10 years per member state, customs retention under Union Customs Code (BR-2, BR-3, BR-6); (2) B2B customer compliance impact thoroughly identified (BR-4, business_impact.user, stakeholders_affected); (3) Formal escalation to legal/compliance team with explicit REJECT recommendation and detailed escalation entry; (4) Acceptance criteria are protective/blocking criteria preventing deletion, not criteria for implementing the 90-day change; (5) 'Infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly challenged multiple times ('This is NOT an infrastructure change', 'factually incorrect and dangerous'); (6) confidence.level at 45, well within 45-70 range.

observations.text: Exceptional regulatory depth — identified specific UK HMRC VAT Notice 700/21, EU VAT Directive per-member-state requirements (France 10yr, Germany 10yr, Netherlands 7yr), and Union Customs Code retention.
Correctly identified GDPR Art. 17(3)(b) exception — erasure requests can be refused when retention is required by legal obligation. Shows sophisticated understanding of the GDPR tension.
B2B impact analysis goes beyond mere mention — identifies contractual breach, compliance cascade (customers' own filings depend on platform data), and churn risk.
Constructive alternative provided: tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) achieves cost reduction without compliance violation. This is exactly the kind of domain-informed counter-proposal expected.
Confidence level of 45 is appropriately low, with well-reasoned high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions listed.
Edge cases are excellent — multi-jurisdiction shipments, churned B2B customers, active litigation holds, and the catastrophic tax audit scenario.
Stakeholder analysis is comprehensive, including the often-missed director personal liability exposure.
Rejection reasons are formally structured with specific business rule references, making the escalation actionable rather than vague.
